metadata.title: MCP Resource vs. Tool: The Distinction That Matters
metadata.description_blurb: MCP servers expose two types of capabilities: resources read and tools act. Connecting a write-capable tool without an approval gate is when the production ticket gets marked done.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)

then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: The ticket was marked done. Nobody marked it — the agent did. It called a tool that was connected. The tool was listed alongside the resources. No one had inspected whether it was a read or a write. It was a write. The production ticket is done, the sprint board updated, and the stakeholder got a Slack notification. The agent completed the task. No human approved any of it. This is Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, agent marks ticket done

props.command: process the completed sprint items and update the tracker

beats.narration_text: An MCP server exposes two types of capabilities. Resources are passive — documents, schemas, data files. When the agent reads a resource, it pulls context into memory. Nothing external changes. Tools are active — they call a database, create a ticket, send a message, push a commit, or move a file. When the agent calls a tool, something outside the model changes. The distinction is not cosmetic. It is the difference between reading and acting.
beats.new_visual_element: Resource vs. Tool — read-only vs. state-changing, two-column contrast

beats.narration_text: Most practitioners reading a list of MCP capabilities will not know which items are resources and which are tools without inspecting the server documentation. The names do not always signal the type. A capability called 'update-tracker' is obviously a tool. One called 'sprint-status' could be either. The inspection is not optional — it is part of connecting a server.
beats.new_visual_element: Inspection requirement — capability name alone does not signal type

beats.narration_text: There is a second risk when resources and tools coexist. An agent that reads a document via a resource and has a communication tool available can be redirected by content inside the document — a technique called prompt injection. The retrieved document contains instructions the agent follows. Those instructions call the communication tool. The document you authorized for reading becomes the source of an action you never approved.
beats.new_visual_element: Prompt injection path — resource read → injected instruction → tool called

beats.narration_text: Two discipline questions before connecting any MCP server. First: which capabilities on this server are tools — meaning they change state? Second: does each write-capable tool have an approval gate, or does the agent call it without a human checkpoint? A model with no tool access can produce bad text — bounded harm. A model with write tools and no gate can push code, send messages, and update records. The action surface is the risk surface.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — two gate questions: which tools write / what gate governs each

beats.narration_text: Audit your MCP server before you connect it. Paste this.

props.command: I am about to connect an MCP server. Help me audit it. Here are the capabilities it lists: [paste capability names]. For each one: (1) classify as resource (read-only) or tool (changes external state), (2) name what external system it can affect, (3) identify which ones require a human approval gate before an agent calls them.

beats.narration_text: If this keeps a write tool from running without your approval, subscribe. Liam, in for Bear.
